quiz_questions.question_text: ST elevation on an ECG indicates:

options.text: A slow heart rate
Acute, complete coronary artery occlusion (STEMI)
A valve problem
A normal heart rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: ST elevation on an ECG is a hallmark finding of an ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), indicating that a coronary artery is completely blocked and transmural myocardial injury is actively occurring.

quiz_questions.question_text: Elevated troponin levels in the blood indicate:

options.text: Normal heart health with intact myocardial tissue integrity
Liver disease with hepatocellular damage and enzyme release
Kidney disease with glomerular filtration impairment only
Myocardial damage causing heart muscle cells to release troponin protein

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac troponin is a highly specific biomarker for myocardial injury; when heart muscle cells are damaged or die, they release troponin into the bloodstream, making it the gold standard blood test for diagnosing myocardial infarction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A normal ejection fraction is approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: The ejection fraction (EF) measures the percentage of blood in the left ventricle that is pumped out with each contraction; a normal EF of 55-70% indicates that the heart is pumping efficiently.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a heart failure case, an EF of 25% indicates:

options.text: Severe systolic dysfunction with significantly reduced pumping ability
Mild, clinically insignificant disease
Normal cardiac function
No heart disease present

quiz_questions.explanation: An ejection fraction of 25% is drastically below the normal range of 55-70%, indicating that the left ventricle is ejecting only a quarter of its blood volume per beat, representing severe heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF).

quiz_questions.question_text: Emergent PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention) for an MI involves:

options.text: Administering intravenous thrombolytic medication only to dissolve the clot without any catheter-based intervention
Heart transplant in which the entire failing heart is surgically replaced with a healthy donor organ
Threading a catheter to the blocked artery, inflating a balloon, and placing a stent to restore blood flow
Open heart surgery with bypass grafts using saphenous vein or internal mammary artery conduits

quiz_questions.explanation: Primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI, involving catheter-based balloon angioplasty to crush the plaque and a stent to hold the artery open, rapidly restoring coronary blood flow and minimizing myocardial damage.

quiz_questions.question_text: B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) is elevated in:

options.text: Heart failure, where stretched ventricles release BNP
Hypertension without heart failure
Myocardial infarction exclusively
Normal health only

quiz_questions.explanation: BNP and NT-proBNP are released by ventricular cardiomyocytes in response to increased wall stress from volume overload; elevated levels are used as a diagnostic biomarker for heart failure and to monitor treatment effectiveness.

quiz_questions.question_text: ACE inhibitors in heart failure provide benefit by:

options.text: Reducing afterload, preventing adverse remodeling, and improving survival
Increasing blood volume to improve filling
Increasing heart rate to compensate for weak pumping
Only lowering blood pressure with no cardiac benefit

quiz_questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors are a cornerstone of heart failure treatment because they reduce afterload through vasodilation, decrease harmful neurohormonal activation, prevent adverse ventricular remodeling, and have been proven to reduce mortality in clinical trials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Diuretics help heart failure patients by:

options.text: Increasing blood volume to improve cardiac output
Raising blood pressure to improve perfusion
Reducing fluid overload by promoting sodium and water excretion, relieving edema and pulmonary congestion
Strengthening heart muscle contraction directly

quiz_questions.explanation: Diuretics relieve the symptoms of fluid overload in heart failure by promoting renal excretion of sodium and water, reducing blood volume and venous pressure, which relieves pulmonary congestion (dyspnea) and peripheral edema.

quiz_questions.question_text: Early recognition of MI symptoms is critical because:

options.text: Earlier treatment saves more heart muscle ('time is muscle')
MI is not a serious condition
MI symptoms always resolve on their own with time
Treatment can safely wait several days

quiz_questions.explanation: During a myocardial infarction, heart muscle dies progressively the longer the artery remains blocked; every minute of delay results in more irreversible muscle loss, so rapid recognition and treatment ('door-to-balloon time') directly determines survival and long-term heart function.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hypertensive emergency requires:

options.text: Taking oral medication at home and resting
Bed rest only
No treatment because high BP always resolves spontaneously
IV antihypertensive medications with careful, controlled BP reduction

quiz_questions.explanation: A hypertensive emergency (severely elevated BP with acute organ damage) requires immediate hospitalization and carefully titrated IV antihypertensives, because too-rapid reduction can cause cerebral or cardiac ischemia.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is echocardiography?

options.text: An X-ray of the chest to visualize the heart
A non-invasive ultrasound imaging technique that assesses heart structure, valve function, and ejection fraction
A blood test that measures cardiac biomarkers
An electrical recording of the heart's rhythm

quiz_questions.explanation: Echocardiography uses ultrasound waves to create real-time images of the heart in motion, allowing clinicians to evaluate chamber size, wall motion, valve function, and calculate ejection fraction without radiation or invasive procedures.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is cardiac rehabilitation?

options.text: A comprehensive program of supervised exercise, education, risk factor modification, and psychological support for cardiac patients
Taking medication only without any lifestyle changes
Complete bed rest for months after a heart attack
A surgical procedure to repair the heart

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac rehabilitation is a structured, multidisciplinary program that combines progressive exercise training, heart-healthy education, nutritional counseling, stress management, and psychological support to help patients recover and reduce the risk of future cardiac events.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is secondary prevention in cardiovascular medicine?

options.text: Screening healthy populations for heart disease
Preventing cardiovascular disease in someone who has never had it
Emergency treatment during an acute MI
Strategies to prevent recurrence of cardiovascular events in patients who already have established disease

quiz_questions.explanation: Secondary prevention refers to interventions (medications, lifestyle changes, rehabilitation) aimed at reducing the risk of future cardiovascular events in patients who have already experienced a heart attack, stroke, or been diagnosed with CAD.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ejection fraction' mean?

options.text: The total volume of blood in the heart
The rate at which the heart beats per minute
The percentage of blood in the left ventricle that is pumped out with each heartbeat
The pressure generated during ventricular contraction

quiz_questions.explanation: Ejection fraction (EF) is calculated as stroke volume divided by end-diastolic volume, expressed as a percentage; it is the single most important measure of left ventricular systolic function and a key prognostic indicator in heart disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is troponin?

options.text: A hormone that regulates heart rate
A cardiac-specific protein released into the blood when heart muscle cells are damaged or dying
A medication used to treat heart failure
A type of cholesterol found in the blood

quiz_questions.explanation: Troponin is a regulatory protein complex in cardiac muscle fibers; cardiac-specific troponin I and T are released into the bloodstream when cardiomyocytes are injured, making them the most sensitive and specific biomarkers for diagnosing myocardial infarction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'interdisciplinary care' mean in cardiac patient management?

options.text: Treatment limited exclusively to surgical and catheter-based interventions performed by interventional cardiologists and cardiac surgeons
A team-based approach involving cardiologists, nurses, pharmacists, dietitians, rehabilitation specialists, and mental health professionals
Care provided solely by a cardiologist who manages all aspects of the patient's cardiac and non-cardiac health independently
Care provided only by the patient's primary care physician without any specialist consultation or multidisciplinary team involvement

quiz_questions.explanation: Interdisciplinary cardiac care involves multiple healthcare professionals collaborating to address all aspects of a patient's cardiovascular condition, from medical management and surgical interventions to nutrition, exercise, medication compliance, and psychological well-being.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is dyspnea?

options.text: Chest pain caused by coronary artery disease
An abnormal heart rhythm
Difficult or labored breathing, often described as shortness of breath
Swelling in the legs and ankles

quiz_questions.explanation: Dyspnea is the subjective sensation of difficulty breathing or air hunger; in the context of heart failure, it typically results from pulmonary congestion when the failing left ventricle allows blood to back up into the lungs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is pedal edema?

options.text: A rash on the feet caused by poor circulation
Swelling of the brain
Swelling of the feet and ankles due to fluid accumulation in the tissues
Pain in the feet during walking

quiz_questions.explanation: Pedal edema refers to swelling in the feet and ankles caused by fluid accumulating in the interstitial tissues; in heart failure, it results from increased venous pressure in right-sided failure or fluid retention, and gravity causes fluid to settle in the lowest body parts.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'STEMI' stand for?

options.text: Standard Treatment for Emergency Myocardial Ischemia
Systolic Tachycardic Emergency Medical Intervention
ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction
Sudden Total Electrical Myocardial Interruption

quiz_questions.explanation: STEMI stands for ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction, a type of heart attack in which complete coronary artery occlusion causes transmural myocardial injury visible as ST-segment elevation on the ECG, requiring emergent reperfusion therapy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'afterload' mean in cardiac physiology?

options.text: The heart rate multiplied by stroke volume
The volume of blood filling the ventricle before contraction
The resistance the ventricle must overcome to eject blood, determined mainly by arterial blood pressure and vascular resistance
The amount of blood remaining in the ventricle after contraction

quiz_questions.explanation: Afterload is the pressure or resistance that the ventricle must generate to open the semilunar valve and eject blood; it is primarily determined by systemic vascular resistance and aortic pressure, and is increased in conditions like hypertension and aortic stenosis.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 58-year-old man arrives at the ER with crushing chest pain for the past 30 minutes. The ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. The cardiologist calls for emergent cardiac catheterization. Which coronary artery is most likely blocked based on these ECG leads?

options.text: Left circumflex artery
Left anterior descending artery
Left main coronary artery
Right coronary artery (inferior wall MI)

quiz_questions.explanation: ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF indicates an inferior wall STEMI, which is most commonly caused by occlusion of the right coronary artery (RCA), as it supplies the inferior portion of the heart including the bottom of the left ventricle.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 72-year-old woman with heart failure is instructed to weigh herself every morning. One day she notices a 4-pound weight gain overnight and calls her cardiologist. Why is this rapid weight gain concerning, and what should the physician likely adjust?

options.text: The weight gain is from eating too much; she should reduce calories
The scale is probably inaccurate; no action is needed
Weight gain is normal in elderly patients and should be ignored
Rapid weight gain indicates fluid retention from worsening heart failure; the physician will likely increase the diuretic dose

quiz_questions.explanation: In heart failure, sudden weight gain of more than 2-3 pounds in a day or 5 pounds in a week typically indicates fluid retention from decompensation; daily weight monitoring is a key self-management tool, and the physician will typically adjust diuretic dosing to remove the excess fluid.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient presents with an acute MI and troponin levels are drawn at arrival and again 6 hours later. The initial troponin is borderline but the 6-hour troponin is significantly elevated. Why are serial troponin measurements important?

options.text: Only one troponin measurement taken at the time of admission is needed to diagnose MI, because troponin levels peak immediately at the onset of chest pain
Troponin levels decrease during an MI because the damaged heart muscle actively reabsorbs the released troponin protein back into the surviving myocardial cells
Troponin levels are always immediately elevated at the exact moment chest pain begins during an MI, so a negative initial test definitively rules out myocardial infarction
Troponin takes time to rise after myocardial injury; serial measurements detect the characteristic rise and fall pattern that confirms MI even when initial levels are normal

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac troponin begins to rise 3-6 hours after myocardial injury, peaks at 12-24 hours, and remains elevated for 7-14 days; serial measurements are critical because the initial draw may be too early to show elevation, and the characteristic rise-and-fall pattern confirms the diagnosis of MI.

quiz_questions.question_text: A heart failure patient on multiple medications reports feeling dizzy when standing. Current medications include an ACE inhibitor, a beta-blocker, and a diuretic. Which medication interaction most likely explains the orthostatic symptoms?

options.text: The combined effect of all three medications lowering blood pressure and reducing heart rate can impair compensatory responses when standing
The diuretic is causing the heart to beat faster
The beta-blocker alone is causing all symptoms
The ACE inhibitor is increasing heart rate too much

quiz_questions.explanation: The ACE inhibitor causes vasodilation, the diuretic reduces blood volume, and the beta-blocker limits the heart's ability to increase rate in response to positional changes; together, these medications can significantly impair the compensatory mechanisms needed to maintain blood pressure when standing.

quiz_questions.question_text: After a successful PCI with stent placement for an acute MI, a patient is prescribed aspirin, clopidogrel, a statin, a beta-blocker, and an ACE inhibitor. The patient asks why so many medications are needed. What is the rationale for this combination?

options.text: Each medication targets a different mechanism: dual antiplatelets prevent stent clotting, the statin reduces cholesterol, the beta-blocker reduces cardiac workload, and the ACE inhibitor prevents remodeling
Only the aspirin is truly necessary after the procedure; the statin, beta-blocker, and ACE inhibitor are all optional supplements that provide no proven cardiovascular benefit
These medications are only needed for the first week after the procedure to prevent immediate complications, and can be safely discontinued once the stent has fully endothelialized
The medications all do the same thing by thinning the blood and reducing clotting risk, making the combination redundant and unnecessarily increasing the risk of bleeding

quiz_questions.explanation: Post-MI secondary prevention requires a multi-drug approach: dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) prevents stent thrombosis, statins lower LDL and stabilize plaques, beta-blockers reduce myocardial oxygen demand and prevent arrhythmias, and ACE inhibitors reduce afterload and prevent harmful ventricular remodeling.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient in the cardiac ICU has an echocardiogram showing an EF of 20% and severe mitral regurgitation. The patient has bilateral lung crackles and oxygen saturation of 88%. What is happening physiologically?

options.text: The severely weakened LV and leaking mitral valve allow blood to back up into the lungs, causing pulmonary edema that impairs gas exchange
The patient has pneumonia unrelated to their heart
The low EF is causing right-sided heart failure only
The patient is hyperventilating from anxiety

quiz_questions.explanation: With an EF of 20%, the LV barely pumps forward; combined with severe mitral regurgitation, blood regurgitates into the left atrium and pulmonary veins during systole, dramatically increasing pulmonary venous pressure and forcing fluid into the alveoli (pulmonary edema), which impairs oxygen exchange.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient who had an MI six months ago is participating in cardiac rehabilitation. During a supervised exercise session, the patient asks whether exercise might cause another heart attack. How should the rehabilitation team respond?

options.text: Exercise is always dangerous after a heart attack and should be avoided permanently
Only bed rest is appropriate for one year after an MI
Supervised, progressive exercise in cardiac rehab is proven to reduce the risk of future cardiac events, improve cardiovascular fitness, and extend life
Intense, unsupervised exercise should begin immediately after hospital discharge

quiz_questions.explanation: Cardiac rehabilitation with supervised, gradually progressive exercise is one of the most evidence-based interventions after an MI; it improves cardiac function, reduces recurrent events by 20-25%, enhances quality of life, and is safe under professional supervision with continuous monitoring.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient presents with BP of 220/130 mmHg, severe headache, blurred vision, and confusion. Labs show elevated creatinine indicating acute kidney injury. Why must blood pressure be lowered carefully rather than rapidly in this hypertensive emergency?

options.text: The heart cannot tolerate any change in blood pressure
The kidneys need high blood pressure to function
Rapid BP reduction has no risks and should always be done
Dropping BP too quickly can cause cerebral ischemia or watershed stroke because the brain has adapted to higher pressures and autoregulation is shifted

quiz_questions.explanation: In chronic hypertension, cerebral autoregulation shifts to maintain blood flow at higher pressures; if BP is dropped too rapidly (more than 25% in the first hour), cerebral perfusion can fall below critical thresholds, causing ischemic stroke, particularly in watershed areas between vascular territories.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 68-year-old patient with heart failure and diabetes is readmitted for the third time in six months for fluid overload. The care team implements a comprehensive discharge plan. Which intervention is most likely to prevent future readmissions?

options.text: Only increasing the diuretic dose without any patient education, as pharmacological management alone is sufficient to prevent all future heart failure readmissions
Comprehensive patient education on daily weight monitoring, medication adherence, sodium restriction, and when to call the doctor, combined with close follow-up
Keeping the patient hospitalized indefinitely until all symptoms resolve completely, as outpatient management of heart failure is unsafe and unreliable
Prescribing stronger pain medication to mask the symptoms of fluid overload, as symptom control is the primary goal and weight monitoring is unnecessary

quiz_questions.explanation: Heart failure readmissions are most effectively prevented through comprehensive patient education (daily weights, fluid/sodium restriction, medication adherence, recognizing warning signs), coordinated follow-up appointments within 7 days of discharge, and addressing comorbidities like diabetes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical student is reviewing a case where a patient with atrial fibrillation developed sudden weakness on one side of the body and difficulty speaking. An MRI confirms an ischemic stroke. How did AFib contribute to this stroke?

options.text: AFib caused a coronary artery to rupture
AFib caused blood pressure to drop, depriving the brain of blood flow
Stagnant blood in the fibrillating atria formed a clot that embolized to the brain, blocking a cerebral artery
AFib caused excessive bleeding in the brain

quiz_questions.explanation: In atrial fibrillation, the non-contracting atria allow blood to stagnate and form thrombi, particularly in the left atrial appendage; these clots can dislodge, travel through the left ventricle and aorta to the cerebral arteries, and occlude a brain vessel causing an ischemic stroke.
